title: Exceptional Collections and the Neron-Severi lattice for surfaces
speaker: Charles Vial

proof_stages.label: Semiorthogonal decompositions and exceptional objects
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up triangulated categories, semiorthogonal decompositions, exceptional objects as fully faithful D^b(k)-summands, exceptional collections, and the admissibility that lets such collections split off orthogonal complements.

proof_stages.label: Exceptional collections as geometric restrictions
proof_stages.summary: Standard full exceptional collections and several restriction mechanisms are reviewed to argue that exceptional collections strongly constrain geometry, through absence of decompositions for Calabi-Yau examples, K_0 and motivic consequences, phantom phenomena, and derived rigidity/Fano criteria.

proof_stages.label: From Euler vanishing to numerical exceptional collections
proof_stages.summary: A projective-space characterization via line-bundle exceptional collections and Riemann-Roch motivates replacing actual Ext-vanishing by Euler-pairing vanishing, leading to numerically exceptional objects, numerically exceptional collections, and maximum length in numerical K-theory.

proof_stages.label: Neron-Severi lattice criterion for surfaces
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: for a smooth projective surface with chi(O_S)=1, existence of a maximum-length numerically exceptional collection is equivalent to K_S^2=10-rank N^1(S) together with explicit unimodularity, parity, and divisibility conditions on the Neron-Severi lattice and K_S.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic and geometric consequences
proof_stages.summary: The criterion implies arithmetic rigidity under field extension and surjectivity of the cycle class map, extends toric-system constructions beyond algebraically closed fields, and yields a complex pg=q=0 classification in which, with the stated Kodaira-dimension-one caveat, exactly the non-Enriques cases admit maximum-length numerical collections.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to rank-one classes and Riemann-Roch lattice
proof_stages.summary: For the hard implication, mutations reduce to numerical classes of rank one, and applying Riemann-Roch to the divisor differences g_i=c_1(E_i)-c_1(E_{i-1}) produces a tridiagonal intersection matrix and an explicit expression for the canonical class.

proof_stages.label: Unimodular lattice linear algebra
proof_stages.summary: Pure integer linear algebra—unimodularity, the Hodge-signature condition, a lemma forcing some diagonal entry a_i to be -1 or 0, and an induction/blowdown computation of a canonical quadratic form—yields the required primitivity/divisibility of K_S and the formula K_S^2=10-rank N^1(S).

examples.name: Beilinson collection on projective space
examples.note: The collection O, O(1), ..., O(n) on P^n makes the definitions of full exceptional collection and semiorthogonal decomposition concrete via line-bundle cohomology.
examples.engagement_reason: the Beilinson collection on projective space is stated as the famous collection and its known semiorthogonality/generation is described but not worked out

passes.reason: The Beilinson collection is the standard full minimal-length line-bundle collection on maximally homogeneous projective space, used as a model example.

passes.reason: The one-point blow-up of P^2 is an ordinary rational surface illustrating Orlov’s blow-up formula, not an adversarial example.

examples.name: Quadrics with spinor-bundle collections
examples.note: Quadrics first illustrate known exceptional collections, and later odd-dimensional quadrics are used as a counterexample showing why the projective-space characterization must assume the exceptional objects are line bundles.
examples.engagement_reason: quadrics are cited with Kapranov's spinor-bundle collections for odd and even dimensions, but no computation or verification is done on them

passes.reason: Smooth quadrics are homogeneous symmetric varieties, and the spinor-bundle collection is invoked to show the line-bundle hypothesis in the projective-space characterization cannot be dropped.
smooth quadrics are highly homogeneous minimal-degree hypersurfaces, and the spinor-bundle collection is later invoked against a line-bundle-free characterization of P^n
Smooth quadrics are homogeneous, and odd-dimensional spinor collections give minimal-length non-projective-space cases showing the line-bundle hypothesis matters.

Smooth quadrics are highly homogeneous maximal-index hypersurfaces, and the odd-dimensional spinor collection shows the line-bundle hypothesis cannot be dropped.

Smooth quadrics are highly homogeneous, and odd quadrics with spinor bundles give a minimal-length exceptional collection showing the line-bundle hypothesis in the P^n characterization is necessary.

Smooth quadrics are homogeneous minimal-degree/high-index Fano varieties, and the odd-dimensional spinor-bundle collections show the line-bundle hypothesis in the P^n criterion cannot simply be dropped.

examples.name: K3/Calabi-Yau surfaces with no nontrivial semiorthogonal decompositions
examples.note: The Calabi-Yau/K3 case illustrates geometric restrictions on semiorthogonal decompositions through Serre duality, connectedness, and skyscraper sheaves.
examples.engagement_reason: for Calabi-Yau/K3-type varieties the speaker proves nonexistence of nontrivial semiorthogonal decompositions using Serre duality, trivial canonical bundle, structure sheaves, and skyscraper sheaves

passes.reason: K3/Calabi-Yau surfaces are used as an obstruction where trivial canonical bundle forces any semiorthogonal decomposition to be trivial.

examples.name: Barlow surface and a phantom category
examples.note: The Barlow surface is used to show that even an integral Tate-type motivic decomposition need not give a full exceptional collection, because a nonzero phantom summand can remain invisible to K_0.
examples.engagement_reason: the Barlow surface is cited as having universally trivial CH0, a Tate motive, and a known exceptional collection with a phantom complement, but these facts are not derived

passes.reason: The Barlow surface is a minimal K^2=1 general-type surface and its nonzero phantom category has vanishing K0, showing motivic/K-theoretic data do not detect fullness.
the Barlow surface is a minimal-general-type example and its nonzero phantom category has collapsed K0, showing numerical or motivic data need not detect fullness

examples.name: Enriques surface obstruction
examples.note: Enriques surfaces illustrate the geometric corollary by giving complex pg=q=0 surfaces for which maximum-length numerical exceptional collections, and hence maximum-length exceptional collections, are obstructed.
examples.engagement_reason: the Enriques case is used in the surface criterion to conclude an obstruction to maximum-length exceptional collections, including the numerical length consequence

passes.reason: The Enriques surface is used as the obstruction ruling out maximum-length numerical exceptional collections, e.g. the expected length 12.
